messages.content: is it correct to put a spec like this: 1 x 8-lane MIPI DSI connector (including 4 lanes multiplexed to LVDS)

Yes, it is correct to specify a connector that has 8 lanes, with 4 of those lanes multiplexed to LVDS. This would typically be used for connecting a display to a device, such as a camera or video processing module. The use of MIPI DSI (Display Serial Interface) technology allows for high-speed data transfer and low power consumption, and the inclusion of LVDS (Low-Voltage Differential Signaling) lanes can provide additional compatibility with certain types of displays.
what's the proper proposition, multiplexed to or multiplexed as

The proper proposition to use in this case is "multiplexed to". This is because the LVDS lanes are being multiplexed onto the 8-lane MIPI DSI connector, rather than being multiplexed as the connector itself. "Multiplexed as" would be more appropriate if the connector itself was being multiplexed to serve some other purpose or function.

Using "multiplexed with LVDS" would also be correct. It would mean that the 8-lane MIPI DSI connector is carrying both MIPI DSI and LVDS signals simultaneously and that these signals are being multiplexed together. This phrase conveys the same meaning as "multiplexed to LVDS."
is it correct?  The VT-SBC-RK66 implements a 4-lane eDP connector (2 × 10 × 2.0mm) for HD cameras via an extension cable.

Yes, the sentence is grammatically correct and makes sense. It describes that the VT-SBC-RK66 has a 4-lane eDP (embedded DisplayPort) connector, which is used for connecting HD cameras via an extension cable. The dimensions of the connector are also specified as 2 × 10 × 2.0mm. "Implements" is used to indicate that the 4-lane eDP connector is integrated into the VT-SBC-RK66 system, enabling it to support connections to HD cameras.

Yes, you can use "offers" instead of "implements" in the sentence. Both words can be used to indicate that the VT-SBC-RK66 has the capability to support a 4-lane eDP connector for HD cameras. For example, "The VT-SBC-RK66 offers a 4-lane eDP connector (2 x 10 x 2.0mm) for HD cameras via an extension cable." In this case, "offers" implies that the feature is available to users, while "implements" implies that it is integrated into the system.
so "implements" means the connector is integrated into the SBC board?

Yes, that's correct. "Implements" means that the feature (in this case, the 4-lane eDP connector for HD cameras) is integrated or built into the design of the VT-SBC-RK66 SBC board. It implies that the feature is an integral part of the design and cannot be easily removed or added later. By contrast, "offers" might suggest that the feature is an optional extra that can be added or removed as needed.
if I need describe that the connector is provided on the board, which one would be better?

If you want to describe that the connector is physically present on the board and available for use, "provides" or "includes" would be good alternatives to "implements." For example, you might say, "The VT-SBC-RK66 includes a 4-lane eDP connector (2 x 10 x 2.0mm) for HD cameras via an extension cable," or "The VT-SBC-RK66 provides a 4-lane eDP connector (2 x 10 x 2.0mm) for HD cameras via an extension cable." These words suggest that the feature is present and available to use but do not indicate whether it is an integral part of the design.
what's the chinese for "implements" in thar context?

The Chinese word for "implements" in this context would be "实现" (shí xiàn). It means to realize, achieve or implement something, so it would be an appropriate word to use if you want to convey that the feature is an integral part of the design and it has been implemented or integrated into the system.

The VT-SBC-RK66 implements four USB 2.0 interfaces (Type-A), including one USB OTG on the top right that can switch between host (down) and device (up) modes with the red switch next to the USB interfaces. Before switching the OTG modes, please position the switch in place before plugging into the USB cable.

The sentence is grammatically correct and clear. It describes that the VT-SBC-RK66 SBC board includes four USB 2.0 interfaces of Type-A, one of which is a USB On-The-Go (OTG) interface located at the top right. The red switch next to the USB interfaces is used to switch between host (down) and device (up) modes. The sentence also provides a cautionary note, advising users to position the switch in place before plugging in the USB cable. Overall, the sentence provides clear technical information and includes important details for users who are using the USB interfaces on the board.
